{{Ancient Japanese Tomb |Name = Muraida Kofun |Image = [[File:Okinaga-no-Misasagi, zankyu.jpg|280px]]
Remains of the mound (Auxiliary area of Okinaga Tomb) |Alias = Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Kofun{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}/Kōgōzuka{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}} |Affiliation = Nagahama Kofun Cluster |Location = {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, Shiga Prefecture, Muraida Aza Kitayashiki |Latitude = 35.4014667 |Longitude = 136.3363611 |ISO = JP-25 |Shape = (Hypothetical) {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tomb|ja|前方後円墳}} |Scale = Unknown |Construction = Mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |Burial Facility = Unknown (contains a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺}}) |Buried Individual = (Designated by {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}} |Artifacts = Crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, mirror, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |Mausoleum = Designated as "Okinaga Tomb" by the Imperial Household Agency |Notes = |Map = Japan Shiga |Label = Muraida Kofun |Icon = |Label Position = top }} [[File:Okinaga-no-Misasagi, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Shrine}}{{small|Relics reburied in Genroku era; mound constructed and designated as a tomb in Meiji era.}}]] '''Muraida Kofun''' (村居田古墳) is an ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, Shiga Prefecture. Its shape is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳}}, and it is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster. The actual individual buried in the tomb remains unclear. However, part of the site has been designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, empress of Emperor Bidatsu, the 30th {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. == Overview == [[File:Kouunji (Maibara), hondou.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple}}{{small|Mound remains are adjacent to the temple's main hall.}}]] Muraida Kofun is situated in the northeastern part of Shiga Prefecture, near the northern end of the Yokoyama Hills in the Hokoku region{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. It is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster (Yokoyama Northern Kofun Cluster), which includes tombs such as the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun and the Kakigome Kofun in Nagahama City{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. According to the *Sakata District Gazetteer* (*Sakata-gunshi*), the mound was discovered in Genroku 9 (1696) during renovations to the main hall of Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple. A stone coffin was unearthed from the mound but later reburied at the current tomb site (designated as Okinaga Tomb). During this discovery, much of the original mound was destroyed{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}, and the remaining parts of the mound are now managed by the Imperial Household Agency. No full-scale investigations have been conducted due to this designation{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The mound's exact shape is unclear due to erosion, but it is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. Surrounding the site, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} (cylindrical haniwa) have been found{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Inside the mound, a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺}} made of Aso welded tuff (known as Aso Pink Stone) was discovered. This coffin, with its four protrusions for rope attachments, represents the easternmost example of such coffins{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Artifacts recorded during the Genroku excavation included a crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, and mirrors. However, these were also reburied alongside the coffin{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The tomb is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, during the mid-Kofun period. It follows the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun chronologically and predates the Kakigome Kofun{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. While it has been designated as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, this attribution is considered unlikely due to discrepancies in the timeline{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. == Excavation History == * Genroku 9 (1696): Discovery of a stone coffin and associated artifacts during temple renovations. Relics reburied at the Hori Residence{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1872 (Meiji 5): Inspection by the Ministry of Education, with sketches made of stone coffin fragments{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1874 (Meiji 7): Official designation as Okinaga Tomb by the Ministry of Education{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1875 (Meiji 8): Mound construction at reburial site, officially recognized as a tomb{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1883 (Meiji 16): Former burial site brought under Imperial Household Agency jurisdiction{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. It is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster (Yokoyama Northern Kofun Cluster), which includes tombs such as the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun and the Kakigome Kofun in Nagahama City{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. According to the *Sakata District Gazetteer* (*Sakata-gunshi*), the mound was discovered in Genroku 9 (1696) during renovations to the main hall of Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple. A stone coffin was unearthed from the mound but later reburied at the current tomb site (designated as Okinaga Tomb). During this discovery, much of the original mound was destroyed{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}, and the remaining parts of the mound are now managed by the Imperial Household Agency. No full-scale investigations have been conducted due to this designation{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The mound's exact shape is unclear due to erosion, but it is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. Surrounding the site, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} (cylindrical haniwa) have been found{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Inside the mound, a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺}} made of Aso welded tuff (known as Aso Pink Stone) was discovered. This coffin, with its four protrusions for rope attachments, represents the easternmost example of such coffins{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Artifacts recorded during the Genroku excavation included a crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, and mirrors. However, these were also reburied alongside the coffin{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The tomb is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, during the mid-Kofun period. It follows the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun chronologically and predates the Kakigome Kofun{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. While it has been designated as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, this attribution is considered unlikely due to discrepancies in the timeline{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. == Excavation History == * Genroku 9 (1696): Discovery of a stone coffin and associated artifacts during temple renovations. Relics reburied at the Hori Residence{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1872 (Meiji 5): Inspection by the Ministry of Education, with sketches made of stone coffin fragments{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1874 (Meiji 7): Official designation as Okinaga Tomb by the Ministry of Education{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1875 (Meiji 8): Mound construction at reburial site, officially recognized as a tomb{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1883 (Meiji 16): Former burial site brought under Imperial Household Agency jurisdiction{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. {{Ancient Japanese Tomb |Name = Muraida Kofun |Image = [[File:Okinaga-no-Misasagi, zankyu.jpg|280px]]
Remains of the mound (Auxiliary area of Okinaga Tomb) |Alias = Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Kofun{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}/Kōgōzuka{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}} |Affiliation = Nagahama Kofun Cluster |Location = {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, Shiga Prefecture, Muraida Aza Kitayashiki |Latitude = 35.4014667 |Longitude = 136.3363611 |ISO = JP-25 |Shape = (Hypothetical) {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tomb|ja|前方後円墳}} |Scale = Unknown |Construction = Mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |Burial Facility = Unknown (contains a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺}}) |Buried Individual = (Designated by {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}} |Artifacts = Crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, mirror, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |Mausoleum = Designated as "Okinaga Tomb" by the Imperial Household Agency |Notes = |Map = Japan Shiga |Label = Muraida Kofun |Icon = |Label Position = top }} [[File:Okinaga-no-Misasagi, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Shrine}}{{small|Relics reburied in Genroku era; mound constructed and designated as a tomb in Meiji era.}}]] '''Muraida Kofun''' (村居田古墳) is an ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, Shiga Prefecture. Its shape is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳}}, and it is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster. The actual individual buried in the tomb remains unclear. However, part of the site has been designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, empress of Emperor Bidatsu, the 30th {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. == Overview == [[File:Kouunji (Maibara), hondou.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple}}{{small|Mound remains are adjacent to the temple's main hall.}}]] Muraida Kofun is situated in the northeastern part of Shiga Prefecture, near the northern end of the Yokoyama Hills in the Hokoku region{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. It is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster (Yokoyama Northern Kofun Cluster), which includes tombs such as the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun and the Kakigome Kofun in Nagahama City{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. According to the *Sakata District Gazetteer* (*Sakata-gunshi*), the mound was discovered in Genroku 9 (1696) during renovations to the main hall of Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple. A stone coffin was unearthed from the mound but later reburied at the current tomb site (designated as Okinaga Tomb). During this discovery, much of the original mound was destroyed{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}, and the remaining parts of the mound are now managed by the Imperial Household Agency. No full-scale investigations have been conducted due to this designation{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The mound's exact shape is unclear due to erosion, but it is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. Surrounding the site, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} (cylindrical haniwa) have been found{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Inside the mound, a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺}} made of Aso welded tuff (known as Aso Pink Stone) was discovered. This coffin, with its four protrusions for rope attachments, represents the easternmost example of such coffins{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Artifacts recorded during the Genroku excavation included a crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, and mirrors. However, these were also reburied alongside the coffin{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The tomb is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, during the mid-Kofun period. It follows the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun chronologically and predates the Kakigome Kofun{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. While it has been designated as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, this attribution is considered unlikely due to discrepancies in the timeline{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. == Excavation History == * Genroku 9 (1696): Discovery of a stone coffin and associated artifacts during temple renovations. Relics reburied at the Hori Residence{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1872 (Meiji 5): Inspection by the Ministry of Education, with sketches made of stone coffin fragments{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1874 (Meiji 7): Official designation as Okinaga Tomb by the Ministry of Education{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1875 (Meiji 8): Mound construction at reburial site, officially recognized as a tomb{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1883 (Meiji 16): Former burial site brought under Imperial Household Agency jurisdiction{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. {{Ancient Japanese Tomb |Name = Muraida Kofun |Image = [[File:Okinaga-no-Misasagi, zankyu.jpg|280px]]
Remains of the mound (Auxiliary area of Okinaga Tomb) |Alias = Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Kofun{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}/Kōgōzuka{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}} |Affiliation = Nagahama Kofun Cluster |Location = {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, Shiga Prefecture, Muraida Aza Kitayashiki |Latitude = 35.4014667 |Longitude = 136.3363611 |ISO = JP-25 |Shape = (Hypothetical) {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tomb|ja|前方後円墳}} |Scale = Unknown |Construction = Mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |Burial Facility = Unknown (contains a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺}}) |Buried Individual = (Designated by {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}} |Artifacts = Crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, mirror, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |Mausoleum = Designated as "Okinaga Tomb" by the Imperial Household Agency |Notes = |Map = Japan Shiga |Label = Muraida Kofun |Icon = |Label Position = top }} [[File:Okinaga-no-Misasagi, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Shrine}}{{small|Relics reburied in Genroku era; mound constructed and designated as a tomb in Meiji era.}}]] '''Muraida Kofun''' (村居田古墳) is an ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, Shiga Prefecture. Its shape is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳}}, and it is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster. The actual individual buried in the tomb remains unclear. However, part of the site has been designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, empress of Emperor Bidatsu, the 30th {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. == Overview == [[File:Kouunji (Maibara), hondou.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple}}{{small|Mound remains are adjacent to the temple's main hall.}}]] Muraida Kofun is situated in the northeastern part of Shiga Prefecture, near the northern end of the Yokoyama Hills in the Hokoku region{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. It is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster (Yokoyama Northern Kofun Cluster), which includes tombs such as the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun and the Kakigome Kofun in Nagahama City{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. According to the *Sakata District Gazetteer* (*Sakata-gunshi*), the mound was discovered in Genroku 9 (1696) during renovations to the main hall of Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple. A stone coffin was unearthed from the mound but later reburied at the current tomb site (designated as Okinaga Tomb). During this discovery, much of the original mound was destroyed{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}, and the remaining parts of the mound are now managed by the Imperial Household Agency. No full-scale investigations have been conducted due to this designation{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The mound's exact shape is unclear due to erosion, but it is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. Surrounding the site, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} (cylindrical haniwa) have been found{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Inside the mound, a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺}} made of Aso welded tuff (known as Aso Pink Stone) was discovered. This coffin, with its four protrusions for rope attachments, represents the easternmost example of such coffins{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Artifacts recorded during the Genroku excavation included a crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, and mirrors. However, these were also reburied alongside the coffin{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The tomb is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, during the mid-Kofun period. It follows the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun chronologically and predates the Kakigome Kofun{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. While it has been designated as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, this attribution is considered unlikely due to discrepancies in the timeline{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. == Excavation History == * Genroku 9 (1696): Discovery of a stone coffin and associated artifacts during temple renovations. Relics reburied at the Hori Residence{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1872 (Meiji 5): Inspection by the Ministry of Education, with sketches made of stone coffin fragments{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1874 (Meiji 7): Official designation as Okinaga Tomb by the Ministry of Education{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1875 (Meiji 8): Mound construction at reburial site, officially recognized as a tomb{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1883 (Meiji 16): Former burial site brought under Imperial Household Agency jurisdiction{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Shosuke Ishida|year=|chapter=Okinaga Tomb|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Era)|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|ref={{Harvid|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Fuminori Sugaya|year=2002|chapter=Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group|title={{ill|Zoku Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|続 日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|isbn=4490105991|ref={{Harvid|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Tetsuro Tsujikawa|year=2006|chapter=Reevaluation of Muraida Kofun in Maibara City|title=Shiga Cultural Properties Protection Association Annual Report Vol. 19|publisher=Shiga Cultural Properties Protection Association|ref={{Harvid|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Chiaki Mizutani|year=2013|chapter=|title=The Mystery of Emperor Keitai and the Korean Peninsula|publisher=Bunshun Shinsho|isbn=978-4166609253|ref={{Harvid|Chiaki Mizutani|2013}}}} == Related Topics == {{Commonscat|Muraida Kofun}} * {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}} revision.text:
{{Ancient Japanese Tomb |Name = Muraida Kofun |Image = [[File:Okinaga-no-Misasagi, zankyu.jpg|280px]]
Remains of the mound (Auxiliary area of Okinaga Tomb) |Alias = Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Kofun{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}/Kōgōzuka{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}} |Affiliation = Nagahama Kofun Cluster |Location = {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, Shiga Prefecture, Muraida Aza Kitayashiki |Latitude = 35.4014667 |Longitude = 136.3363611 |ISO = JP-25 |Shape = (Hypothetical) {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tomb|ja|前方後円墳}} |Scale = Unknown |Construction = Mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |Burial Facility = Unknown (contains a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺}}) |Buried Individual = (Designated by {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}} |Artifacts = Crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, mirror, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |Mausoleum = Designated as "Okinaga Tomb" by the Imperial Household Agency |Notes = |Map = Japan Shiga |Label = Muraida Kofun |Icon = |Label Position = top }} [[File:Okinaga-no-Misasagi, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Shrine}}{{small|Relics reburied in Genroku era; mound constructed and designated as a tomb in Meiji era.}}]] '''Muraida Kofun''' (村居田古墳) is an ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, Shiga Prefecture. Its shape is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳}}, and it is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster. The actual individual buried in the tomb remains unclear. However, part of the site has been designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, empress of Emperor Bidatsu, the 30th {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. == Overview == [[File:Kouunji (Maibara), hondou.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple}}{{small|Mound remains are adjacent to the temple's main hall.}}]] Muraida Kofun is situated in the northeastern part of Shiga Prefecture, near the northern end of the Yokoyama Hills in the Hokoku region{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. It is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster (Yokoyama Northern Kofun Cluster), which includes tombs such as the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun and the Kakigome Kofun in Nagahama City{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. According to the *Sakata District Gazetteer* (*Sakata-gunshi*), the mound was discovered in Genroku 9 (1696) during renovations to the main hall of Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple. A stone coffin was unearthed from the mound but later reburied at the current tomb site (designated as Okinaga Tomb). During this discovery, much of the original mound was destroyed{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}, and the remaining parts of the mound are now managed by the Imperial Household Agency. No full-scale investigations have been conducted due to this designation{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The mound's exact shape is unclear due to erosion, but it is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. Surrounding the site, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} (cylindrical haniwa) have been found{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Inside the mound, a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺}} made of Aso welded tuff (known as Aso Pink Stone) was discovered. This coffin, with its four protrusions for rope attachments, represents the easternmost example of such coffins{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Artifacts recorded during the Genroku excavation included a crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, and mirrors. However, these were also reburied alongside the coffin{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The tomb is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, during the mid-Kofun period. It follows the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun chronologically and predates the Kakigome Kofun{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. While it has been designated as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, this attribution is considered unlikely due to discrepancies in the timeline{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. == Excavation History == * Genroku 9 (1696): Discovery of a stone coffin and associated artifacts during temple renovations. Relics reburied at the Hori Residence{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1872 (Meiji 5): Inspection by the Ministry of Education, with sketches made of stone coffin fragments{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1874 (Meiji 7): Official designation as Okinaga Tomb by the Ministry of Education{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1875 (Meiji 8): Mound construction at reburial site, officially recognized as a tomb{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1883 (Meiji 16): Former burial site brought under Imperial Household Agency jurisdiction{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Shosuke Ishida|year=|chapter=Okinaga Tomb|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Era)|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|ref={{Harvid|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Fuminori Sugaya|year=2002|chapter=Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group|title={{ill|Zoku Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|続 日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|isbn=4490105991|ref={{Harvid|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Tetsuro Tsujikawa|year=2006|chapter=Reevaluation of Muraida Kofun in Maibara City|title=Shiga Cultural Properties Protection Association Annual Report Vol. 19|publisher=Shiga Cultural Properties Protection Association|ref={{Harvid|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Chiaki Mizutani|year=2013|chapter=|title=The Mystery of Emperor Keitai and the Korean Peninsula|publisher=Bunshun Shinsho|isbn=978-4166609253|ref={{Harvid|Chiaki Mizutani|2013}}}} == Related Topics == {{Commonscat|Muraida Kofun}} * {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}} revision.comment: Adding [[Category:前方後円墳]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:前方後円墳]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Ancient Japanese Tomb |Name = Muraida Kofun |image = [[File:Okinaga-no-Misasagi, zankyu.jpg|280px]]
Remains of the mound (Auxiliary area of Okinaga Tomb) |Alias = Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Kofun{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}/Kōgōzuka{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}} |Affiliation = Nagahama Kofun Cluster |Location = {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, Shiga Prefecture, Muraida Aza Kitayashiki |Latitude = 35.4014667 |Longitude = 136.3363611 |ISO = JP-25 |Shape = (Hypothetical) {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tomb|ja|前方後円墳}} |Scale = Unknown |Construction = Mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |Burial Facility = Unknown (contains a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺}}) |Buried Individual = (Designated by {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}} |Artifacts = Crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, mirror, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |Mausoleum = Designated as "Okinaga Tomb" by the Imperial Household Agency |Notes = |Map = Japan Shiga |Label = Muraida Kofun |Icon = |Label Position = top }} [[File:Okinaga-no-Misasagi, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Shrine}}{{small|Relics reburied in Genroku era; mound constructed and designated as a tomb in Meiji era.}}]] '''Muraida Kofun''' (村居田古墳) is an ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, Shiga Prefecture. Its shape is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳}}, and it is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster. The actual individual buried in the tomb remains unclear. However, part of the site has been designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, empress of Emperor Bidatsu, the 30th {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. == Overview == [[File:Kouunji (Maibara), hondou.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple}}{{small|Mound remains are adjacent to the temple's main hall.}}]] Muraida Kofun is situated in the northeastern part of Shiga Prefecture, near the northern end of the Yokoyama Hills in the Hokoku region{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. It is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster (Yokoyama Northern Kofun Cluster), which includes tombs such as the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun and the Kakigome Kofun in Nagahama City{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. According to the *Sakata District Gazetteer* (*Sakata-gunshi*), the mound was discovered in Genroku 9 (1696) during renovations to the main hall of Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple. A stone coffin was unearthed from the mound but later reburied at the current tomb site (designated as Okinaga Tomb). During this discovery, much of the original mound was destroyed{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}, and the remaining parts of the mound are now managed by the Imperial Household Agency. No full-scale investigations have been conducted due to this designation{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The mound's exact shape is unclear due to erosion, but it is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. Surrounding the site, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} (cylindrical haniwa) have been found{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Inside the mound, a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺}} made of Aso welded tuff (known as Aso Pink Stone) was discovered. This coffin, with its four protrusions for rope attachments, represents the easternmost example of such coffins{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Artifacts recorded during the Genroku excavation included a crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, and mirrors. However, these were also reburied alongside the coffin{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The tomb is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, during the mid-Kofun period. It follows the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun chronologically and predates the Kakigome Kofun{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. While it has been designated as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, this attribution is considered unlikely due to discrepancies in the timeline{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. == Excavation History == * Genroku 9 (1696): Discovery of a stone coffin and associated artifacts during temple renovations. Relics reburied at the Hori Residence{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1872 (Meiji 5): Inspection by the Ministry of Education, with sketches made of stone coffin fragments{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1874 (Meiji 7): Official designation as Okinaga Tomb by the Ministry of Education{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1875 (Meiji 8): Mound construction at reburial site, officially recognized as a tomb{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1883 (Meiji 16): Former burial site brought under Imperial Household Agency jurisdiction{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Shosuke Ishida|year=|chapter=Okinaga Tomb|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Era)|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|ref={{Harvid|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Fuminori Sugaya|year=2002|chapter=Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group|title={{ill|Zoku Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|続 日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|isbn=4490105991|ref={{Harvid|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Tetsuro Tsujikawa|year=2006|chapter=Reevaluation of Muraida Kofun in Maibara City|title=Shiga Cultural Properties Protection Association Annual Report Vol. 19|publisher=Shiga Cultural Properties Protection Association|ref={{Harvid|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Chiaki Mizutani|year=2013|chapter=|title=The Mystery of Emperor Keitai and the Korean Peninsula|publisher=Bunshun Shinsho|isbn=978-4166609253|ref={{Harvid|Chiaki Mizutani|2013}}}} == Related Topics == {{Commonscat|Muraida Kofun}} * {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}} {{Ancient Japanese Tomb |Name = Muraida Kofun |image = [[File:Okinaga-no-Misasagi, zankyu.jpg|280px]]
Remains of the mound (Auxiliary area of Okinaga Tomb) |Alias = Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Kofun{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}/Kōgōzuka{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}} |Affiliation = Nagahama Kofun Cluster |Location = {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, Shiga Prefecture, Muraida Aza Kitayashiki |Latitude = 35.4014667 |Longitude = 136.3363611 |ISO = JP-25 |Shape = (Hypothetical) {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tomb|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} |Scale = Unknown |Construction = Mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |Burial Facility = Unknown (contains a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺}}) |Buried Individual = (Designated by {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}} |Artifacts = Crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, mirror, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |Mausoleum = Designated as "Okinaga Tomb" by the Imperial Household Agency |Notes = |Map = Japan Shiga |Label = Muraida Kofun |Icon = |Label Position = top }} [[File:Okinaga-no-Misasagi, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Shrine}}{{small|Relics reburied in Genroku era; mound constructed and designated as a tomb in Meiji era.}}]] '''Muraida Kofun''' (村居田古墳) is an ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, Shiga Prefecture. Its shape is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, and it is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster. The actual individual buried in the tomb remains unclear. However, part of the site has been designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, empress of Emperor Bidatsu, the 30th {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. == Overview == [[File:Kouunji (Maibara), hondou.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple}}{{small|Mound remains are adjacent to the temple's main hall.}}]] Muraida Kofun is situated in the northeastern part of Shiga Prefecture, near the northern end of the Yokoyama Hills in the Hokoku region{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. It is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster (Yokoyama Northern Kofun Cluster), which includes tombs such as the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun and the Kakigome Kofun in Nagahama City{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. According to the *Sakata District Gazetteer* (*Sakata-gunshi*), the mound was discovered in Genroku 9 (1696) during renovations to the main hall of Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple. A stone coffin was unearthed from the mound but later reburied at the current tomb site (designated as Okinaga Tomb). During this discovery, much of the original mound was destroyed{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}, and the remaining parts of the mound are now managed by the Imperial Household Agency. No full-scale investigations have been conducted due to this designation{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The mound's exact shape is unclear due to erosion, but it is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. Surrounding the site, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} (cylindrical haniwa) have been found{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Inside the mound, a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺}} made of Aso welded tuff (known as Aso Pink Stone) was discovered. This coffin, with its four protrusions for rope attachments, represents the easternmost example of such coffins{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Artifacts recorded during the Genroku excavation included a crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, and mirrors. However, these were also reburied alongside the coffin{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The tomb is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, during the mid-Kofun period. It follows the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun chronologically and predates the Kakigome Kofun{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. While it has been designated as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, this attribution is considered unlikely due to discrepancies in the timeline{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. == Excavation History == * Genroku 9 (1696): Discovery of a stone coffin and associated artifacts during temple renovations. Relics reburied at the Hori Residence{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1872 (Meiji 5): Inspection by the Ministry of Education, with sketches made of stone coffin fragments{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1874 (Meiji 7): Official designation as Okinaga Tomb by the Ministry of Education{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1875 (Meiji 8): Mound construction at reburial site, officially recognized as a tomb{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1883 (Meiji 16): Former burial site brought under Imperial Household Agency jurisdiction{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. {{Ancient Japanese Tomb |Name = Muraida Kofun |image = [[File:Okinaga-no-Misasagi, zankyu.jpg|280px]]
Remains of the mound (Auxiliary area of Okinaga Tomb) |Alias = Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Kofun{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}/Kōgōzuka{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}} |Affiliation = Nagahama Kofun Cluster |Location = {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, Shiga Prefecture, Muraida Aza Kitayashiki |Latitude = 35.4014667 |Longitude = 136.3363611 |ISO = JP-25 |Shape = (Hypothetical) {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tomb|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} |Scale = Unknown |Construction = Mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |Burial Facility = Unknown (contains a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺}}) |Buried Individual = (Designated by {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}} |Artifacts = Crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, mirror, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |Mausoleum = Designated as "Okinaga Tomb" by the Imperial Household Agency |Notes = |Map = Japan Shiga |Label = Muraida Kofun |Icon = |Label Position = top }} [[File:Okinaga-no-Misasagi, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Shrine}}{{small|Relics reburied in Genroku era; mound constructed and designated as a tomb in Meiji era.}}]] '''Muraida Kofun''' (村居田古墳) is an ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, Shiga Prefecture. Its shape is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, and it is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster. The actual individual buried in the tomb remains unclear. However, part of the site has been designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, empress of Emperor Bidatsu, the 30th {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. == Overview == [[File:Kouunji (Maibara), hondou.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple}}{{small|Mound remains are adjacent to the temple's main hall.}}]] Muraida Kofun is situated in the northeastern part of Shiga Prefecture, near the northern end of the Yokoyama Hills in the Hokoku region{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. It is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster (Yokoyama Northern Kofun Cluster), which includes tombs such as the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun and the Kakigome Kofun in Nagahama City{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. According to the *Sakata District Gazetteer* (*Sakata-gunshi*), the mound was discovered in Genroku 9 (1696) during renovations to the main hall of Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple. A stone coffin was unearthed from the mound but later reburied at the current tomb site (designated as Okinaga Tomb). During this discovery, much of the original mound was destroyed{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}, and the remaining parts of the mound are now managed by the Imperial Household Agency. No full-scale investigations have been conducted due to this designation{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The mound's exact shape is unclear due to erosion, but it is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. Surrounding the site, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} (cylindrical haniwa) have been found{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Inside the mound, a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺}} made of Aso welded tuff (known as Aso Pink Stone) was discovered. This coffin, with its four protrusions for rope attachments, represents the easternmost example of such coffins{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Artifacts recorded during the Genroku excavation included a crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, and mirrors. However, these were also reburied alongside the coffin{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The tomb is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, during the mid-Kofun period. It follows the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun chronologically and predates the Kakigome Kofun{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. While it has been designated as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, this attribution is considered unlikely due to discrepancies in the timeline{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. == Excavation History == * Genroku 9 (1696): Discovery of a stone coffin and associated artifacts during temple renovations. Relics reburied at the Hori Residence{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1872 (Meiji 5): Inspection by the Ministry of Education, with sketches made of stone coffin fragments{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1874 (Meiji 7): Official designation as Okinaga Tomb by the Ministry of Education{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1875 (Meiji 8): Mound construction at reburial site, officially recognized as a tomb{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1883 (Meiji 16): Former burial site brought under Imperial Household Agency jurisdiction{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Shosuke Ishida|year=|chapter=Okinaga Tomb|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Era)|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|ref={{Harvid|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Fuminori Sugaya|year=2002|chapter=Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group|title={{ill|Zoku Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|続 日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|isbn=4490105991|ref={{Harvid|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Tetsuro Tsujikawa|year=2006|chapter=Reevaluation of Muraida Kofun in Maibara City|title=Shiga Cultural Properties Protection Association Annual Report Vol. 19|publisher=Shiga Cultural Properties Protection Association|ref={{Harvid|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Chiaki Mizutani|year=2013|chapter=|title=The Mystery of Emperor Keitai and the Korean Peninsula|publisher=Bunshun Shinsho|isbn=978-4166609253|ref={{Harvid|Chiaki Mizutani|2013}}}} == Related Topics == {{Commonscat|Muraida Kofun}} * {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}} {{Ancient Japanese Tomb |Name = Muraida Kofun |image = [[File:Okinaga-no-Misasagi, zankyu.jpg|280px]]
Remains of the mound (Auxiliary area of Okinaga Tomb) |Alias = Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Kofun{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}/Kōgōzuka{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}} |Affiliation = Nagahama Kofun Cluster |Location = {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, Shiga Prefecture, Muraida Aza Kitayashiki |Latitude = 35.4014667 |Longitude = 136.3363611 |ISO = JP-25 |Shape = (Hypothetical) {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tomb|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} |Scale = Unknown |Construction = Mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |Burial Facility = Unknown (contains a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}}) |Buried Individual = (Designated by {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}} |Artifacts = Crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, mirror, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |Mausoleum = Designated as "Okinaga Tomb" by the Imperial Household Agency |Notes = |Map = Japan Shiga |Label = Muraida Kofun |Icon = |Label Position = top }} [[File:Okinaga-no-Misasagi, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Shrine}}{{small|Relics reburied in Genroku era; mound constructed and designated as a tomb in Meiji era.}}]] '''Muraida Kofun''' (村居田古墳) is an ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, Shiga Prefecture. Its shape is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, and it is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster. The actual individual buried in the tomb remains unclear. However, part of the site has been designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, empress of Emperor Bidatsu, the 30th {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. == Overview == [[File:Kouunji (Maibara), hondou.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple}}{{small|Mound remains are adjacent to the temple's main hall.}}]] Muraida Kofun is situated in the northeastern part of Shiga Prefecture, near the northern end of the Yokoyama Hills in the Hokoku region{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. It is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster (Yokoyama Northern Kofun Cluster), which includes tombs such as the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun and the Kakigome Kofun in Nagahama City{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. According to the *Sakata District Gazetteer* (*Sakata-gunshi*), the mound was discovered in Genroku 9 (1696) during renovations to the main hall of Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple. A stone coffin was unearthed from the mound but later reburied at the current tomb site (designated as Okinaga Tomb). During this discovery, much of the original mound was destroyed{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}, and the remaining parts of the mound are now managed by the Imperial Household Agency. No full-scale investigations have been conducted due to this designation{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The mound's exact shape is unclear due to erosion, but it is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. Surrounding the site, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} (cylindrical haniwa) have been found{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Inside the mound, a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}} made of Aso welded tuff (known as Aso Pink Stone) was discovered. This coffin, with its four protrusions for rope attachments, represents the easternmost example of such coffins{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Artifacts recorded during the Genroku excavation included a crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, and mirrors. However, these were also reburied alongside the coffin{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The tomb is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, during the mid-Kofun period. It follows the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun chronologically and predates the Kakigome Kofun{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. While it has been designated as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, this attribution is considered unlikely due to discrepancies in the timeline{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. == Excavation History == * Genroku 9 (1696): Discovery of a stone coffin and associated artifacts during temple renovations. Relics reburied at the Hori Residence{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1872 (Meiji 5): Inspection by the Ministry of Education, with sketches made of stone coffin fragments{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1874 (Meiji 7): Official designation as Okinaga Tomb by the Ministry of Education{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1875 (Meiji 8): Mound construction at reburial site, officially recognized as a tomb{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1883 (Meiji 16): Former burial site brought under Imperial Household Agency jurisdiction{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. fragments{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1874 (Meiji 7): Official designation as Okinaga Tomb by the Ministry of Education{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1875 (Meiji 8): Mound construction at reburial site, officially recognized as a tomb{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1883 (Meiji 16): Former burial site brought under Imperial Household Agency jurisdiction{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. {{Ancient Japanese Tomb |Name = Muraida Kofun |image = [[File:Okinaga-no-Misasagi, zankyu.jpg|280px]]
Remains of the mound (Auxiliary area of Okinaga Tomb) |Alias = Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Kofun{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}/Kōgōzuka{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}} |Affiliation = Nagahama Kofun Cluster |Location = {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, Shiga Prefecture, Muraida Aza Kitayashiki |Latitude = 35.4014667 |Longitude = 136.3363611 |ISO = JP-25 |Shape = (Hypothetical) {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tomb|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} |Scale = Unknown |Construction = Mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |Burial Facility = Unknown (contains a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}}) |Buried Individual = (Designated by {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}} |Artifacts = Crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, mirror, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |Mausoleum = Designated as "Okinaga Tomb" by the Imperial Household Agency |Notes = |Map = Japan Shiga |Label = Muraida Kofun |Icon = |Label Position = top }} [[File:Okinaga-no-Misasagi, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Shrine}}{{small|Relics reburied in Genroku era; mound constructed and designated as a tomb in Meiji era.}}]] '''Muraida Kofun''' (村居田古墳) is an ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, Shiga Prefecture. Its shape is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, and it is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster. The actual individual buried in the tomb remains unclear. However, part of the site has been designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, empress of Emperor Bidatsu, the 30th {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. == Overview == [[File:Kouunji (Maibara), hondou.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple}}{{small|Mound remains are adjacent to the temple's main hall.}}]] Muraida Kofun is situated in the northeastern part of Shiga Prefecture, near the northern end of the Yokoyama Hills in the Hokoku region{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. It is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster (Yokoyama Northern Kofun Cluster), which includes tombs such as the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun and the Kakigome Kofun in Nagahama City{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. According to the *Sakata District Gazetteer* (*Sakata-gunshi*), the mound was discovered in Genroku 9 (1696) during renovations to the main hall of Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple. A stone coffin was unearthed from the mound but later reburied at the current tomb site (designated as Okinaga Tomb). During this discovery, much of the original mound was destroyed{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}, and the remaining parts of the mound are now managed by the Imperial Household Agency. No full-scale investigations have been conducted due to this designation{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The mound's exact shape is unclear due to erosion, but it is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. Surrounding the site, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} (cylindrical haniwa) have been found{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Inside the mound, a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}} made of Aso welded tuff (known as Aso Pink Stone) was discovered. This coffin, with its four protrusions for rope attachments, represents the easternmost example of such coffins{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Artifacts recorded during the Genroku excavation included a crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, and mirrors. However, these were also reburied alongside the coffin{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The tomb is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, during the mid-Kofun period. It follows the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun chronologically and predates the Kakigome Kofun{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. While it has been designated as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, this attribution is considered unlikely due to discrepancies in the timeline{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. == Excavation History == * Genroku 9 (1696): Discovery of a stone coffin and associated artifacts during temple renovations. Relics reburied at the Hori Residence{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1872 (Meiji 5): Inspection by the Ministry of Education, with sketches made of stone coffin fragments{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1874 (Meiji 7): Official designation as Okinaga Tomb by the Ministry of Education{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1875 (Meiji 8): Mound construction at reburial site, officially recognized as a tomb{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1883 (Meiji 16): Former burial site brought under Imperial Household Agency jurisdiction{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. {{Ancient Japanese Tomb |Name = Muraida Kofun |image = [[File:Okinaga-no-Misasagi, zankyu.jpg|280px]]
Remains of the mound (Auxiliary area of Okinaga Tomb) |Alias = Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Kofun{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}/Kōgōzuka{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}} |Affiliation = Nagahama Kofun Cluster |Location = {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, Shiga Prefecture, Muraida Aza Kitayashiki |Latitude = 35.4014667 |Longitude = 136.3363611 |ISO = JP-25 |Shape = (Hypothetical) {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tomb|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} |Scale = Unknown |Construction = Mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |Burial Facility = Unknown (contains a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}}) |Buried Individual = (Designated by {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}} |Artifacts = Crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, mirror, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |Mausoleum = Designated as "Okinaga Tomb" by the Imperial Household Agency |Notes = |Map = Japan Shiga |Label = Muraida Kofun |Icon = |Label Position = top }} [[File:Okinaga-no-Misasagi, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Shrine}}{{small|Relics reburied in Genroku era; mound constructed and designated as a tomb in Meiji era.}}]] '''Muraida Kofun''' (村居田古墳) is an ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, Shiga Prefecture. Its shape is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, and it is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster. The actual individual buried in the tomb remains unclear. However, part of the site has been designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, empress of Emperor Bidatsu, the 30th {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. == Overview == [[File:Kouunji (Maibara), hondou.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple}}{{small|Mound remains are adjacent to the temple's main hall.}}]] Muraida Kofun is situated in the northeastern part of Shiga Prefecture, near the northern end of the Yokoyama Hills in the Hokoku region{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. It is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster (Yokoyama Northern Kofun Cluster), which includes tombs such as the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun and the Kakigome Kofun in Nagahama City{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. According to the *Sakata District Gazetteer* (*Sakata-gunshi*), the mound was discovered in Genroku 9 (1696) during renovations to the main hall of Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple. A stone coffin was unearthed from the mound but later reburied at the current tomb site (designated as Okinaga Tomb). During this discovery, much of the original mound was destroyed{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}, and the remaining parts of the mound are now managed by the Imperial Household Agency. No full-scale investigations have been conducted due to this designation{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The mound's exact shape is unclear due to erosion, but it is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. Surrounding the site, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} (cylindrical haniwa) have been found{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Inside the mound, a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}} made of Aso welded tuff (known as Aso Pink Stone) was discovered. This coffin, with its four protrusions for rope attachments, represents the easternmost example of such coffins{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Artifacts recorded during the Genroku excavation included a crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, and mirrors. However, these were also reburied alongside the coffin{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The tomb is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, during the mid-Kofun period. It follows the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun chronologically and predates the Kakigome Kofun{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. While it has been designated as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, this attribution is considered unlikely due to discrepancies in the timeline{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. == Excavation History == * Genroku 9 (1696): Discovery of a stone coffin and associated artifacts during temple renovations. Relics reburied at the Hori Residence{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1872 (Meiji 5): Inspection by the Ministry of Education, with sketches made of stone coffin fragments{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1874 (Meiji 7): Official designation as Okinaga Tomb by the Ministry of Education{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1875 (Meiji 8): Mound construction at reburial site, officially recognized as a tomb{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1883 (Meiji 16): Former burial site brought under Imperial Household Agency jurisdiction{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Inside the mound, a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}} made of Aso welded tuff (known as Aso Pink Stone) was discovered. This coffin, with its four protrusions for rope attachments, represents the easternmost example of such coffins{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Artifacts recorded during the Genroku excavation included a crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, and mirrors. However, these were also reburied alongside the coffin{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The tomb is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, during the mid-Kofun period. It follows the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun chronologically and predates the Kakigome Kofun{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. While it has been designated as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, this attribution is considered unlikely due to discrepancies in the timeline{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. == Excavation History == * Genroku 9 (1696): Discovery of a stone coffin and associated artifacts during temple renovations. Relics reburied at the Hori Residence{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1872 (Meiji 5): Inspection by the Ministry of Education, with sketches made of stone coffin fragments{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1874 (Meiji 7): Official designation as Okinaga Tomb by the Ministry of Education{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1875 (Meiji 8): Mound construction at reburial site, officially recognized as a tomb{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1883 (Meiji 16): Former burial site brought under Imperial Household Agency jurisdiction{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Shosuke Ishida|year=|chapter=Okinaga Tomb|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Era)|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|ref={{Harvid|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Fuminori Sugaya|year=2002|chapter=Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group|title={{ill|Zoku Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|続 日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|isbn=4490105991|ref={{Harvid|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Tetsuro Tsujikawa|year=2006|chapter=Reevaluation of Muraida Kofun in Maibara City|title=Shiga Cultural Properties Protection Association Annual Report Vol. 19|publisher=Shiga Cultural Properties Protection Association|ref={{Harvid|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Chiaki Mizutani|year=2013|chapter=|title=The Mystery of Emperor Keitai and the Korean Peninsula|publisher=Bunshun Shinsho|isbn=978-4166609253|ref={{Harvid|Chiaki Mizutani|2013}}}} == See Also == {{Commonscat|Muraida Kofun}} * {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Kofun in {{Ancient Japanese Tomb |Name = Muraida Kofun |image = [[File:Okinaga-no-Misasagi, zankyu.jpg|280px]]
Remains of the mound (Auxiliary area of Okinaga Tomb) |Alias = Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Kofun{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}/Kōgōzuka{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}} |Affiliation = Nagahama Kofun Cluster |Location = {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, Shiga Prefecture, Muraida Aza Kitayashiki |Latitude = 35.4014667 |Longitude = 136.3363611 |ISO = JP-25 |Shape = (Hypothetical) {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tomb|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} |Scale = Unknown |Construction = Mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |Burial Facility = Unknown (contains a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}}) |Buried Individual = (Designated by {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}} |Artifacts = Crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, mirror, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |Mausoleum = Designated as "Okinaga Tomb" by the Imperial Household Agency |Notes = |Map = Japan Shiga |Label = Muraida Kofun |Icon = |Label Position = top }} [[File:Okinaga-no-Misasagi, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Shrine}}{{small|Relics reburied in Genroku era; mound constructed and designated as a tomb in Meiji era.}}]] '''Muraida Kofun''' (村居田古墳) is an ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, Shiga Prefecture. Its shape is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, and it is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster. The actual individual buried in the tomb remains unclear. However, part of the site has been designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, empress of Emperor Bidatsu, the 30th {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. == Overview == [[File:Kouunji (Maibara), hondou.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple}}{{small|Mound remains are adjacent to the temple's main hall.}}]] Muraida Kofun is situated in the northeastern part of Shiga Prefecture, near the northern end of the Yokoyama Hills in the Hokoku region{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. It is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster (Yokoyama Northern Kofun Cluster), which includes tombs such as the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun and the Kakigome Kofun in Nagahama City{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. According to the *Sakata District Gazetteer* (*Sakata-gunshi*), the mound was discovered in Genroku 9 (1696) during renovations to the main hall of Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple. A stone coffin was unearthed from the mound but later reburied at the current tomb site (designated as Okinaga Tomb). During this discovery, much of the original mound was destroyed{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}, and the remaining parts of the mound are now managed by the Imperial Household Agency. No full-scale investigations have been conducted due to this designation{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The mound's exact shape is unclear due to erosion, but it is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. Surrounding the site, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} (cylindrical haniwa) have been found{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Inside the mound, a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}} made of Aso welded tuff (known as Aso Pink Stone) was discovered. This coffin, with its four protrusions for rope attachments, represents the easternmost example of such coffins{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Artifacts recorded during the Genroku excavation included a crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, and mirrors. However, these were also reburied alongside the coffin{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The tomb is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, during the mid-Kofun period. It follows the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun chronologically and predates the Kakigome Kofun{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. While it has been designated as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, this attribution is considered unlikely due to discrepancies in the timeline{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. == Excavation History == * Genroku 9 (1696): Discovery of a stone coffin and associated artifacts during temple renovations. Relics reburied at the Hori Residence{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1872 (Meiji 5): Inspection by the Ministry of Education, with sketches made of stone coffin fragments{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1874 (Meiji 7): Official designation as Okinaga Tomb by the Ministry of Education{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1875 (Meiji 8): Mound construction at reburial site, officially recognized as a tomb{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1883 (Meiji 16): Former burial site brought under Imperial Household Agency jurisdiction{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Shosuke Ishida|year=|chapter=Okinaga Tomb|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Era)|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|ref={{Harvid|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Fuminori Sugaya|year=2002|chapter=Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group|title={{ill|Zoku Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|続 日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|isbn=4490105991|ref={{Harvid|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Tetsuro Tsujikawa|year=2006|chapter=Reevaluation of Muraida Kofun in Maibara City|title=Shiga Cultural Properties Protection Association Annual Report Vol. 19|publisher=Shiga Cultural Properties Protection Association|ref={{Harvid|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Chiaki Mizutani|year=2013|chapter=|title=The Mystery of Emperor Keitai and the Korean Peninsula|publisher=Bunshun Shinsho|isbn=978-4166609253|ref={{Harvid|Chiaki Mizutani|2013}}}} == See Also == {{Commonscat|Muraida Kofun}} * {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Kofun in {{Ancient Japanese Tomb |Name = Muraida Kofun |image = [[File:Okinaga-no-Misasagi, zankyu.jpg|280px]]
Remains of the mound (Auxiliary area of Okinaga Tomb) |Alias = Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Kofun{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}/Kōgōzuka{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}} |Affiliation = Nagahama Kofun Cluster |Location = {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, Shiga Prefecture, Muraida Aza Kitayashiki |Latitude = 35.4014667 |Longitude = 136.3363611 |ISO = JP-25 |Shape = (Hypothetical) {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tomb|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} |Scale = Unknown |Construction = Mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |Burial Facility = Unknown (contains a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}}) |Buried Individual = (Designated by {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}} |Artifacts = Crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, mirror, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |Mausoleum = Designated as "Okinaga Tomb" by the Imperial Household Agency |Notes = |Map = Japan Shiga |Label = Muraida Kofun |Icon = |Label Position = top }} [[File:Okinaga-no-Misasagi, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Shrine}}{{small|Relics reburied in Genroku era; mound constructed and designated as a tomb in Meiji era.}}]] '''Muraida Kofun''' (村居田古墳) is an ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, Shiga Prefecture. Its shape is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, and it is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster. The actual individual buried in the tomb remains unclear. However, part of the site has been designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, empress of Emperor Bidatsu, the 30th {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. == Overview == [[File:Kouunji (Maibara), hondou.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple}}{{small|Mound remains are adjacent to the temple's main hall.}}]] Muraida Kofun is situated in the northeastern part of Shiga Prefecture, near the northern end of the Yokoyama Hills in the Hokoku region{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. It is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster (Yokoyama Northern Kofun Cluster), which includes tombs such as the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun and the Kakigome Kofun in Nagahama City{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. According to the *Sakata District Gazetteer* (*Sakata-gunshi*), the mound was discovered in Genroku 9 (1696) during renovations to the main hall of Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple. A stone coffin was unearthed from the mound but later reburied at the current tomb site (designated as Okinaga Tomb). During this discovery, much of the original mound was destroyed{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}, and the remaining parts of the mound are now managed by the Imperial Household Agency. No full-scale investigations have been conducted due to this designation{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The mound's exact shape is unclear due to erosion, but it is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. Surrounding the site, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} (cylindrical haniwa) have been found{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Inside the mound, a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}} made of Aso welded tuff (known as Aso Pink Stone) was discovered. This coffin, with its four protrusions for rope attachments, represents the easternmost example of such coffins{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Artifacts recorded during the Genroku excavation included a crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, and mirrors. However, these were also reburied alongside the coffin{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The tomb is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, during the mid-Kofun period. It follows the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun chronologically and predates the Kakigome Kofun{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. While it has been designated as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, this attribution is considered unlikely due to discrepancies in the timeline{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. == Excavation History == * Genroku 9 (1696): Discovery of a stone coffin and associated artifacts during temple renovations. Relics reburied at the Hori Residence{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1872 (Meiji 5): Inspection by the Ministry of Education, with sketches made of stone coffin fragments{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1874 (Meiji 7): Official designation as Okinaga Tomb by the Ministry of Education{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1875 (Meiji 8): Mound construction at reburial site, officially recognized as a tomb{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1883 (Meiji 16): Former burial site brought under Imperial Household Agency jurisdiction{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|ref={{Harvid|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Fuminori Sugaya|year=2002|chapter=Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group|title={{ill|Zoku Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|続 日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|isbn=4490105991|ref={{Harvid|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Tetsuro Tsujikawa|year=2006|chapter=Reevaluation of Muraida Kofun in Maibara City|title=Shiga Cultural Properties Protection Association Annual Report Vol. 19|publisher=Shiga Cultural Properties Protection Association|ref={{Harvid|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Chiaki Mizutani|year=2013|chapter=|title=The Mystery of Emperor Keitai and the Korean Peninsula|publisher=Bunshun Shinsho|isbn=978-4166609253|ref={{Harvid|Chiaki Mizutani|2013}}}} == See Also == {{Commonscat|Muraida Kofun}} * {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Kofun in revision.text:
{{Ancient Japanese Tomb |Name = Muraida Kofun |image = [[File:Okinaga-no-Misasagi, zankyu.jpg|280px]]
Remains of the mound (Auxiliary area of Okinaga Tomb) |Alias = Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Kofun{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}/Kōgōzuka{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}} |Affiliation = Nagahama Kofun Cluster |Location = {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, Shiga Prefecture, Muraida Aza Kitayashiki |Latitude = 35.4014667 |Longitude = 136.3363611 |ISO = JP-25 |Shape = (Hypothetical) {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tomb|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} |Scale = Unknown |Construction = Mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |Burial Facility = Unknown (contains a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}}) |Buried Individual = (Designated by {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}} |Artifacts = Crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, mirror, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |Mausoleum = Designated as "Okinaga Tomb" by the Imperial Household Agency |Notes = |Map = Japan Shiga |Label = Muraida Kofun |Icon = |Label Position = top }} [[File:Okinaga-no-Misasagi, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Shrine}}{{small|Relics reburied in Genroku era; mound constructed and designated as a tomb in Meiji era.}}]] '''Muraida Kofun''' (村居田古墳) is an ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, Shiga Prefecture. Its shape is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, and it is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster. The actual individual buried in the tomb remains unclear. However, part of the site has been designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, empress of Emperor Bidatsu, the 30th {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. == Overview == [[File:Kouunji (Maibara), hondou.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple}}{{small|Mound remains are adjacent to the temple's main hall.}}]] Muraida Kofun is situated in the northeastern part of Shiga Prefecture, near the northern end of the Yokoyama Hills in the Hokoku region{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. It is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster (Yokoyama Northern Kofun Cluster), which includes tombs such as the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun and the Kakigome Kofun in Nagahama City{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. According to the *Sakata District Gazetteer* (*Sakata-gunshi*), the mound was discovered in Genroku 9 (1696) during renovations to the main hall of Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple. A stone coffin was unearthed from the mound but later reburied at the current tomb site (designated as Okinaga Tomb). During this discovery, much of the original mound was destroyed{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}, and the remaining parts of the mound are now managed by the Imperial Household Agency. No full-scale investigations have been conducted due to this designation{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The mound's exact shape is unclear due to erosion, but it is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. Surrounding the site, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} (cylindrical haniwa) have been found{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Inside the mound, a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}} made of Aso welded tuff (known as Aso Pink Stone) was discovered. This coffin, with its four protrusions for rope attachments, represents the easternmost example of such coffins{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Artifacts recorded during the Genroku excavation included a crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, and mirrors. However, these were also reburied alongside the coffin{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The tomb is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, during the mid-Kofun period. It follows the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun chronologically and predates the Kakigome Kofun{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. While it has been designated as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, this attribution is considered unlikely due to discrepancies in the timeline{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. == Excavation History == * Genroku 9 (1696): Discovery of a stone coffin and associated artifacts during temple renovations. Relics reburied at the Hori Residence{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1872 (Meiji 5): Inspection by the Ministry of Education, with sketches made of stone coffin fragments{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1874 (Meiji 7): Official designation as Okinaga Tomb by the Ministry of Education{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1875 (Meiji 8): Mound construction at reburial site, officially recognized as a tomb{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1883 (Meiji 16): Former burial site brought under Imperial Household Agency jurisdiction{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Shosuke Ishida|year=|chapter=Okinaga Tomb|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Era)|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|ref={{Harvid|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Fuminori Sugaya|year=2002|chapter=Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group|title={{ill|Zoku Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|続 日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|isbn=4490105991|ref={{Harvid|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Tetsuro Tsujikawa|year=2006|chapter=Reevaluation of Muraida Kofun in Maibara City|title=Shiga Cultural Properties Protection Association Annual Report Vol. 19|publisher=Shiga Cultural Properties Protection Association|ref={{Harvid|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Chiaki Mizutani|year=2013|chapter=|title=The Mystery of Emperor Keitai and the Korean Peninsula|publisher=Bunshun Shinsho|isbn=978-4166609253|ref={{Harvid|Chiaki Mizutani|2013}}}} == See Also == {{Commonscat|Muraida Kofun}} * {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Kofun in Shiga Prefecture]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:History of Maibara]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] reburial site, officially recognized as a tomb{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1883 (Meiji 16): Former burial site brought under Imperial Household Agency jurisdiction{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}.
