revision.comment: another mass replace to make the parameters work correctly
revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=now we have a 'Burial Facility' and 'burial goods' parameter. So a lot of these pages might not be marked on our wiki as having unknown parameters. But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:02, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones[[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Ancient Japanese Tomb |Name = Muraida Kofun |image = [[File:Okinaga-no-Misasagi, zankyu.jpg|280px]]
Remains of the mound (Auxiliary area of Okinaga Tomb) |Alias = Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Kofun{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}/Kōgōzuka{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}} |Affiliation = Nagahama Kofun Cluster |Location = {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, Shiga Prefecture, Muraida Aza Kitayashiki |Latitude = 35.4014667 |Longitude = 136.3363611 |ISO = JP-25 |Shape = (Hypothetical) {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tomb|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} |map_size = Unknown |Construction = Mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |Burial Facility = Unknown (contains a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}}) |Buried Individual = (Designated by {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}} |burial goods = Crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, mirror, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |Mausoleum = Designated as "Okinaga Tomb" by the Imperial Household Agency |Notes = |map = Japan Shiga |map_dot_label = Muraida Kofun |Icon = |map_dot_label Position = top }} [[File:Okinaga-no-Misasagi, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Shrine}}{{small|Relics reburied in Genroku era; mound constructed and designated as a tomb in Meiji era.}}]] '''Muraida Kofun''' (村居田古墳) is an ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, Shiga Prefecture. Its shape is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, and it is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster. The actual individual buried in the tomb remains unclear. However, part of the site has been designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, empress of Emperor Bidatsu, the 30th {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. == Overview == [[File:Kouunji (Maibara), hondou.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple}}{{small|Mound remains are adjacent to the temple's main hall.}}]] Muraida Kofun is situated in the northeastern part of Shiga Prefecture, near the northern end of the Yokoyama Hills in the Hokoku region{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. It is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster (Yokoyama Northern Kofun Cluster), which includes tombs such as the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun and the Kakigome Kofun in Nagahama City{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. According to the *Sakata District Gazetteer* (*Sakata-gunshi*), the mound was discovered in Genroku 9 (1696) during renovations to the main hall of Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple. A stone coffin was unearthed from the mound but later reburied at the current tomb site (designated as Okinaga Tomb). During this discovery, much of the original mound was destroyed{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}, and the remaining parts of the mound are now managed by the Imperial Household Agency. No full-map_size investigations have been conducted due to this designation{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The mound's exact shape is unclear due to erosion, but it is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. Surrounding the site, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} (cylindrical haniwa) have been found{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Inside the mound, a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}} made of Aso welded tuff (known as Aso Pink Stone) was discovered. This coffin, with its four protrusions for rope attachments, represents the easternmost example of such coffins{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Artifacts recorded during the Genroku excavation included a crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, and mirrors. However, these were also reburied alongside the coffin{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The tomb is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, during the mid-Kofun period. It follows the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun chronologically and predates the Kakigome Kofun{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. While it has been designated as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, this attribution is considered unlikely due to discrepancies in the timeline{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. == Excavation History == * Genroku 9 (1696): Discovery of a stone coffin and associated artifacts during temple renovations. Relics reburied at the Hori Residence{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1872 (Meiji 5): Inspection by the Ministry of Education, with sketches made of stone coffin fragments{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1874 (Meiji 7): Official designation as Okinaga Tomb by the Ministry of Education{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1875 (Meiji 8): Mound construction at reburial site, officially recognized as a tomb{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1883 (Meiji 16): Former burial site brought under Imperial Household Agency jurisdiction{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Shosuke Ishida|year=|chapter=Okinaga Tomb|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Era)|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|ref={{Harvid|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Fuminori Sugaya|year=2002|chapter=Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group|title={{ill|Zoku Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|続 日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|isbn=4490105991|ref={{Harvid|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Tetsuro Tsujikawa|year=2006|chapter=Reevaluation of Muraida Kofun in Maibara City|title=Shiga Cultural Properties Protection Association Annual Report Vol. 19|publisher=Shiga Cultural Properties Protection Association|ref={{Harvid|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Chiaki Mizutani|year=2013|chapter=|title=The Mystery of Emperor Keitai and the Korean Peninsula|publisher=Bunshun Shinsho|isbn=978-4166609253|ref={{Harvid|Chiaki Mizutani|2013}}}} == See Also == {{Commonscat|Muraida Kofun}} * {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}} [[Category:Kofun in Shiga Prefecture]] [[Category:History of The mound's exact shape is unclear due to erosion, but it is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. Surrounding the site, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} (cylindrical haniwa) have been found{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Inside the mound, a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}} made of Aso welded tuff (known as Aso Pink Stone) was discovered. This coffin, with its four protrusions for rope attachments, represents the easternmost example of such coffins{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Artifacts recorded during the Genroku excavation included a crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, and mirrors. However, these were also reburied alongside the coffin{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The tomb is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, during the mid-Kofun period. It follows the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun chronologically and predates the Kakigome Kofun{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. While it has been designated as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, this attribution is considered unlikely due to discrepancies in the timeline{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. == Excavation History == * Genroku 9 (1696): Discovery of a stone coffin and associated artifacts during temple renovations. Relics reburied at the Hori Residence{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1872 (Meiji 5): Inspection by the Ministry of Education, with sketches made of stone coffin fragments{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1874 (Meiji 7): Official designation as Okinaga Tomb by the Ministry of Education{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1875 (Meiji 8): Mound construction at reburial site, officially recognized as a tomb{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1883 (Meiji 16): Former burial site brought under Imperial Household Agency jurisdiction{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Its shape is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, and it is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster. The actual individual buried in the tomb remains unclear. However, part of the site has been designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, empress of Emperor Bidatsu, the 30th {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. == Overview == [[File:Kouunji (Maibara), hondou.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple}}{{small|Mound remains are adjacent to the temple's main hall.}}]] Muraida Kofun is situated in the northeastern part of Shiga Prefecture, near the northern end of the Yokoyama Hills in the Hokoku region{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. It is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster (Yokoyama Northern Kofun Cluster), which includes tombs such as the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun and the Kakigome Kofun in Nagahama City{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. According to the *Sakata District Gazetteer* (*Sakata-gunshi*), the mound was discovered in Genroku 9 (1696) during renovations to the main hall of Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple. A stone coffin was unearthed from the mound but later reburied at the current tomb site (designated as Okinaga Tomb). During this discovery, much of the original mound was destroyed{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}, and the remaining parts of the mound are now managed by the Imperial Household Agency. No full-map_size investigations have been conducted due to this designation{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The mound's exact shape is unclear due to erosion, but it is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. Surrounding the site, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} (cylindrical haniwa) have been found{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Inside the mound, a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}} made of Aso welded tuff (known as Aso Pink Stone) was discovered. This coffin, with its four protrusions for rope attachments, represents the easternmost example of such coffins{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Artifacts recorded during the Genroku excavation included a crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, and mirrors. However, these were also reburied alongside the coffin{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The tomb is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, during the mid-Kofun period. It follows the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun chronologically and predates the Kakigome Kofun{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. While it has been designated as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, this attribution is considered unlikely due to discrepancies in the timeline{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. == Excavation History == * Genroku 9 (1696): Discovery of a stone coffin and associated artifacts during temple renovations. Relics reburied at the Hori Residence{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1872 (Meiji 5): Inspection by the Ministry of Education, with sketches made of stone coffin fragments{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1874 (Meiji 7): Official designation as Okinaga Tomb by the Ministry of Education{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1875 (Meiji 8): Mound construction at reburial site, officially recognized as a tomb{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1883 (Meiji 16): Former burial site brought under Imperial Household Agency jurisdiction{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Remains of the mound (Auxiliary area of Okinaga Tomb) |Alias = Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Kofun{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}/Kōgōzuka{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}} |Affiliation = Nagahama Kofun Cluster |Location = {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, Shiga Prefecture, Muraida Aza Kitayashiki |coordinates = 35.4014667 |coordinates = 136.3363611 |ISO = JP-25 |type = (Hypothetical) {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tomb|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} |map_size = Unknown |Construction = Mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |Burial Facility = Unknown (contains a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}}) |Buried Individual = (Designated by {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}} |burial goods = Crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, mirror, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |Mausoleum = Designated as "Okinaga Tomb" by the Imperial Household Agency |Notes = |map = Japan Shiga |map_dot_label = Muraida Kofun |Icon = |map_dot_label = top }} [[File:Okinaga-no-Misasagi, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Shrine}}{{small|Relics reburied in Genroku era; mound constructed and designated as a tomb in Meiji era.}}]] '''Muraida Kofun''' (村居田古墳) is an ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, Shiga Prefecture. Its shape is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, and it is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster. The actual individual buried in the tomb remains unclear. However, part of the site has been designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, empress of Emperor Bidatsu, the 30th {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. == Overview == [[File:Kouunji (Maibara), hondou.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple}}{{small|Mound remains are adjacent to the temple's main hall.}}]] Muraida Kofun is situated in the northeastern part of Shiga Prefecture, near the northern end of the Yokoyama Hills in the Hokoku region{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. It is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster (Yokoyama Northern Kofun Cluster), which includes tombs such as the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun and the Kakigome Kofun in Nagahama City{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. According to the *Sakata District Gazetteer* (*Sakata-gunshi*), the mound was discovered in Genroku 9 (1696) during renovations to the main hall of Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple. A stone coffin was unearthed from the mound but later reburied at the current tomb site (designated as Okinaga Tomb). During this discovery, much of the original mound was destroyed{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}, and the remaining parts of the mound are now managed by the Imperial Household Agency. No full-map_size investigations have been conducted due to this designation{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The mound's exact shape is unclear due to erosion, but it is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. Surrounding the site, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} (cylindrical haniwa) have been found{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Inside the mound, a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}} made of Aso welded tuff (known as Aso Pink Stone) was discovered. This coffin, with its four protrusions for rope attachments, represents the easternmost example of such coffins{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Artifacts recorded during the Genroku excavation included a crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, and mirrors. However, these were also reburied alongside the coffin{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The tomb is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, during the mid-Kofun period. It follows the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun chronologically and predates the Kakigome Kofun{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. While it has been designated as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, this attribution is considered unlikely due to discrepancies in the timeline{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. == Excavation History == * Genroku 9 (1696): Discovery of a stone coffin and associated artifacts during temple renovations. Relics reburied at the Hori Residence{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1872 (Meiji 5): Inspection by the Ministry of Education, with sketches made of stone coffin fragments{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1874 (Meiji 7): Official designation as Okinaga Tomb by the Ministry of Education{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1875 (Meiji 8): Mound construction at reburial site, officially recognized as a tomb{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1883 (Meiji 16): Former burial site brought under Imperial Household Agency jurisdiction{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. The mound's exact type is unclear due to erosion, but it is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. Surrounding the site, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} (cylindrical haniwa) have been found{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Inside the mound, a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}} made of Aso welded tuff (known as Aso Pink Stone) was discovered. This coffin, with its four protrusions for rope attachments, represents the easternmost example of such coffins{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Artifacts recorded during the Genroku excavation included a crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, and mirrors. However, these were also reburied alongside the coffin{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The tomb is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, during the mid-Kofun period. It follows the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun chronologically and predates the Kakigome Kofun{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. While it has been designated as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, this attribution is considered unlikely due to discrepancies in the timeline{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. == Excavation History == * Genroku 9 (1696): Discovery of a stone coffin and associated artifacts during temple renovations. Relics reburied at the Hori Residence{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1872 (Meiji 5): Inspection by the Ministry of Education, with sketches made of stone coffin fragments{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1874 (Meiji 7): Official designation as Okinaga Tomb by the Ministry of Education{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1875 (Meiji 8): Mound construction at reburial site, officially recognized as a tomb{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1883 (Meiji 16): Former burial site brought under Imperial Household Agency jurisdiction{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Remains of the mound (Auxiliary area of Okinaga Tomb) |Alias = Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Kofun{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}/Kōgōzuka{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}} |Affiliation = Nagahama Kofun Cluster |Location = {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, Shiga Prefecture, Muraida Aza Kitayashiki |coordinates = 35.4014667 |coordinates = 136.3363611 |ISO = JP-25 |type = (Hypothetical) {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tomb|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} |map_size = Unknown |Construction = Mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |Burial Facility = Unknown (contains a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}}) |Buried Individual = (Designated by {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}} |burial goods = Crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, mirror, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |Mausoleum = Designated as "Okinaga Tomb" by the Imperial Household Agency |Notes = |map = Japan Shiga |map_dot_label = Muraida Kofun |Icon = |map_dot_label = top }} [[File:Okinaga-no-Misasagi, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Shrine}}{{small|Relics reburied in Genroku era; mound constructed and designated as a tomb in Meiji era.}}]] '''Muraida Kofun''' (村居田古墳) is an ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, Shiga Prefecture. Its type is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, and it is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster. The actual individual buried in the tomb remains unclear. However, part of the site has been designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, empress of Emperor Bidatsu, the 30th {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. == Overview == [[File:Kouunji (Maibara), hondou.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple}}{{small|Mound remains are adjacent to the temple's main hall.}}]] Muraida Kofun is situated in the northeastern part of Shiga Prefecture, near the northern end of the Yokoyama Hills in the Hokoku region{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. It is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster (Yokoyama Northern Kofun Cluster), which includes tombs such as the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun and the Kakigome Kofun in Nagahama City{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. According to the *Sakata District Gazetteer* (*Sakata-gunshi*), the mound was discovered in Genroku 9 (1696) during renovations to the main hall of Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple. A stone coffin was unearthed from the mound but later reburied at the current tomb site (designated as Okinaga Tomb). During this discovery, much of the original mound was destroyed{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}, and the remaining parts of the mound are now managed by the Imperial Household Agency. No full-map_size investigations have been conducted due to this designation{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The mound's exact type is unclear due to erosion, but it is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. Surrounding the site, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} (cylindrical haniwa) have been found{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Inside the mound, a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}} made of Aso welded tuff (known as Aso Pink Stone) was discovered. This coffin, with its four protrusions for rope attachments, represents the easternmost example of such coffins{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Artifacts recorded during the Genroku excavation included a crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, and mirrors. However, these were also reburied alongside the coffin{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The tomb is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, during the mid-Kofun period. It follows the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun chronologically and predates the Kakigome Kofun{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. While it has been designated as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, this attribution is considered unlikely due to discrepancies in the timeline{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. == Excavation History == * Genroku 9 (1696): Discovery of a stone coffin and associated artifacts during temple renovations. Relics reburied at the Hori Residence{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1872 (Meiji 5): Inspection by the Ministry of Education, with sketches made of stone coffin fragments{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1874 (Meiji 7): Official designation as Okinaga Tomb by the Ministry of Education{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1875 (Meiji 8): Mound construction at reburial site, officially recognized as a tomb{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1883 (Meiji 16): Former burial site brought under Imperial Household Agency jurisdiction{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Remains of the mound (Auxiliary area of Okinaga Tomb) |Alias = Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Kofun{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}/Kōgōzuka{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}} |Affiliation = Nagahama Kofun Cluster |Location = {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, Shiga Prefecture, Muraida Aza Kitayashiki |coordinates = 35.4014667 |coordinates = 136.3363611 |ISO = JP-25 |type = (Hypothetical) {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tomb|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} |map_size = Unknown |Construction = Mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |burial = Unknown (contains a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}}) |Buried Individual = (Designated by {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}} |burial goods = Crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, mirror, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |Mausoleum = Designated as "Okinaga Tomb" by the Imperial Household Agency |Notes = |map = Japan Shiga |map_dot_label = Muraida Kofun |Icon = |map_dot_label = top }} [[File:Okinaga-no-Misasagi, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Shrine}}{{small|Relics reburied in Genroku era; mound constructed and designated as a tomb in Meiji era.}}]] '''Muraida Kofun''' (村居田古墳) is an ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, Shiga Prefecture. Its type is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, and it is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster. The actual individual buried in the tomb remains unclear. However, part of the site has been designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, empress of Emperor Bidatsu, the 30th {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. == Overview == [[File:Kouunji (Maibara), hondou.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple}}{{small|Mound remains are adjacent to the temple's main hall.}}]] Muraida Kofun is situated in the northeastern part of Shiga Prefecture, near the northern end of the Yokoyama Hills in the Hokoku region{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. It is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster (Yokoyama Northern Kofun Cluster), which includes tombs such as the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun and the Kakigome Kofun in Nagahama City{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. According to the *Sakata District Gazetteer* (*Sakata-gunshi*), the mound was discovered in Genroku 9 (1696) during renovations to the main hall of Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple. A stone coffin was unearthed from the mound but later reburied at the current tomb site (designated as Okinaga Tomb). During this discovery, much of the original mound was destroyed{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}, and the remaining parts of the mound are now managed by the Imperial Household Agency. No full-map_size investigations have been conducted due to this designation{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The mound's exact type is unclear due to erosion, but it is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. Surrounding the site, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} (cylindrical haniwa) have been found{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Inside the mound, a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}} made of Aso welded tuff (known as Aso Pink Stone) was discovered. This coffin, with its four protrusions for rope attachments, represents the easternmost example of such coffins{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Artifacts recorded during the Genroku excavation included a crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, and mirrors. However, these were also reburied alongside the coffin{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The tomb is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, during the mid-Kofun period. It follows the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun chronologically and predates the Kakigome Kofun{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. While it has been designated as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, this attribution is considered unlikely due to discrepancies in the timeline{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. == Excavation History == * Genroku 9 (1696): Discovery of a stone coffin and associated artifacts during temple renovations. Relics reburied at the Hori Residence{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1872 (Meiji 5): Inspection by the Ministry of Education, with sketches made of stone coffin fragments{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1874 (Meiji 7): Official designation as Okinaga Tomb by the Ministry of Education{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1875 (Meiji 8): Mound construction at reburial site, officially recognized as a tomb{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1883 (Meiji 16): Former burial site brought under Imperial Household Agency jurisdiction{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. It is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster (Yokoyama Northern Kofun Cluster), which includes tombs such as the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun and the Kakigome Kofun in Nagahama City{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. According to the *Sakata District Gazetteer* (*Sakata-gunshi*), the mound was discovered in Genroku 9 (1696) during renovations to the main hall of Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple. A stone coffin was unearthed from the mound but later reburied at the current tomb site (designated as Okinaga Tomb). During this discovery, much of the original mound was destroyed{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}, and the remaining parts of the mound are now managed by the Imperial Household Agency. No full-map_size investigations have been conducted due to this designation{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The mound's exact type is unclear due to erosion, but it is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. Surrounding the site, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} (cylindrical haniwa) have been found{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Inside the mound, a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}} made of Aso welded tuff (known as Aso Pink Stone) was discovered. This coffin, with its four protrusions for rope attachments, represents the easternmost example of such coffins{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Artifacts recorded during the Genroku excavation included a crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, and mirrors. However, these were also reburied alongside the coffin{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The tomb is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, during the mid-Kofun period. It follows the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun chronologically and predates the Kakigome Kofun{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. While it has been designated as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, this attribution is considered unlikely due to discrepancies in the timeline{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. == Excavation History == * Genroku 9 (1696): Discovery of a stone coffin and associated artifacts during temple renovations. Relics reburied at the Hori Residence{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1872 (Meiji 5): Inspection by the Ministry of Education, with sketches made of stone coffin fragments{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1874 (Meiji 7): Official designation as Okinaga Tomb by the Ministry of Education{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1875 (Meiji 8): Mound construction at reburial site, officially recognized as a tomb{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1883 (Meiji 16): Former burial site brought under Imperial Household Agency jurisdiction{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=trimming these off. the parameter works as "burial" so no need to use "Burial Facility"[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:35, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=added parameters Interred_individual and Mausoleum is planned to be added but I messed up in template editing[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:40, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=now we have a 'burial' and 'burial goods' parameter. So a lot of these pages might not be marked on our wiki as having unknown parameters. But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:02, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones[[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Ancient Japanese Tomb |name = Muraida Kofun |image = [[File:Okinaga-no-Misasagi, zankyu.jpg|280px]]
Remains of the mound (Auxiliary area of Okinaga Tomb) |alternate_name = Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Kofun{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}/Kōgōzuka{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}} |part_of = Nagahama Kofun Cluster |location = {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, Shiga Prefecture, Muraida Aza Kitayashiki |coordinates = 35.4014667 |coordinates = 136.3363611 |ISO = JP-25 |type = (Hypothetical) {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tomb|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} |map_size = Unknown |Construction = Mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |burial = Unknown (contains a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}}) |Buried Individual = (Designated by {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}} |burial goods = Crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, mirror, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |Mausoleum = Designated as "Okinaga Tomb" by the Imperial Household Agency |Notes = |map = Japan Shiga |map_dot_label = Muraida Kofun |Icon = |map_dot_label = top }} [[File:Okinaga-no-Misasagi, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Shrine}}{{small|Relics reburied in Genroku era; mound constructed and designated as a tomb in Meiji era.}}]] '''Muraida Kofun''' (村居田古墳) is an ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, Shiga Prefecture. Its type is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, and it is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster. The actual individual buried in the tomb remains unclear. However, part of the site has been designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, empress of Emperor Bidatsu, the 30th {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. == Overview == [[File:Kouunji (Maibara), hondou.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple}}{{small|Mound remains are adjacent to the temple's main hall.}}]] Muraida Kofun is situated in the northeastern part of Shiga Prefecture, near the northern end of the Yokoyama Hills in the Hokoku region{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. It is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster (Yokoyama Northern Kofun Cluster), which includes tombs such as the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun and the Kakigome Kofun in Nagahama City{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. According to the *Sakata District Gazetteer* (*Sakata-gunshi*), the mound was discovered in Genroku 9 (1696) during renovations to the main hall of Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple. A stone coffin was unearthed from the mound but later reburied at the current tomb site (designated as Okinaga Tomb). During this discovery, much of the original mound was destroyed{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}, and the remaining parts of the mound are now managed by the Imperial Household Agency. No full-map_size investigations have been conducted due to this designation{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The mound's exact type is unclear due to erosion, but it is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. Surrounding the site, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} (cylindrical haniwa) have been found{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Inside the mound, a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}} made of Aso welded tuff (known as Aso Pink Stone) was discovered. This coffin, with its four protrusions for rope attachments, represents the easternmost example of such coffins{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Artifacts recorded during the Genroku excavation included a crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, and mirrors. However, these were also reburied alongside the coffin{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The tomb is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, during the mid-Kofun period. It follows the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun chronologically and predates the Kakigome Kofun{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. While it has been designated as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, this attribution is considered unlikely due to discrepancies in the timeline{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. == Excavation History == * Genroku 9 (1696): Discovery of a stone coffin and associated artifacts during temple renovations. Relics reburied at the Hori Residence{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1872 (Meiji 5): Inspection by the Ministry of Education, with sketches made of stone coffin fragments{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1874 (Meiji 7): Official designation as Okinaga Tomb by the Ministry of Education{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1875 (Meiji 8): Mound construction at reburial site, officially recognized as a tomb{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1883 (Meiji 16): Former burial site brought under Imperial Household Agency jurisdiction{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Shosuke Ishida|year=|chapter=Okinaga Tomb|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Era)|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|ref={{Harvid|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Fuminori Sugaya|year=2002|chapter=Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group|title={{ill|Zoku Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|続 日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|isbn=4490105991|ref={{Harvid|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Tetsuro Tsujikawa|year=2006|chapter=Reevaluation of Muraida Kofun in Maibara City|title=Shiga other_designation Protection Association Annual Report Vol. 19|publisher=Shiga other_designation Protection Association|ref={{Harvid|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Chiaki Mizutani|year=2013|chapter=|title=The Mystery of Emperor Keitai and the Korean Peninsula|publisher=Bunshun Shinsho|isbn=978-4166609253|ref={{Harvid|Chiaki Mizutani|2013}}}} == See Also == {{Commonscat|Muraida Kofun}} * {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}} [[Category:Kofun in Shiga Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Kofun]][[sn:Muraita Kofun]][[sn:Muraita Kofun]][[sn:Muraita Kofun]][[Category:11]][[sn:Muraita Kofun]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Muraita Kofun]][[Category:11]] Remains of the mound (Auxiliary area of Okinaga Tomb) |alternate_name = Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Kofun{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}/Kōgōzuka{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}} |part_of = Nagahama Kofun Cluster |location = {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, Shiga Prefecture, Muraida Aza Kitayashiki |coordinates = 35.4014667 |coordinates = 136.3363611 |omitted = JP-25 |type = (Hypothetical) {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tomb|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} |map_size = Unknown |Construction = Mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |burial = Unknown (contains a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}}) |Buried Individual = (Designated by {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}} |burial goods = Crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, mirror, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |Mausoleum = Designated as "Okinaga Tomb" by the Imperial Household Agency |Notes = |map = Japan Shiga |map_dot_label = Muraida Kofun |type = |map_dot_label = top }} [[File:Okinaga-no-Misasagi, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Shrine}}{{small|Relics reburied in Genroku era; mound constructed and designated as a tomb in Meiji era.}}]] '''Muraida Kofun''' (村居田古墳) is an ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, Shiga Prefecture. Its type is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, and it is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster. The actual individual buried in the tomb remains unclear. However, part of the site has been designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, empress of Emperor Bidatsu, the 30th {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. == Overview == [[File:Kouunji (Maibara), hondou.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple}}{{small|Mound remains are adjacent to the temple's main hall.}}]] Muraida Kofun is situated in the northeastern part of Shiga Prefecture, near the northern end of the Yokoyama Hills in the Hokoku region{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. It is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster (Yokoyama Northern Kofun Cluster), which includes tombs such as the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun and the Kakigome Kofun in Nagahama City{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. According to the *Sakata District Gazetteer* (*Sakata-gunshi*), the mound was discovered in Genroku 9 (1696) during renovations to the main hall of Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple. A stone coffin was unearthed from the mound but later reburied at the current tomb site (designated as Okinaga Tomb). During this discovery, much of the original mound was destroyed{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}, and the remaining parts of the mound are now managed by the Imperial Household Agency. No full-map_size investigations have been conducted due to this designation{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The mound's exact type is unclear due to erosion, but it is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. Surrounding the site, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} (cylindrical haniwa) have been found{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Inside the mound, a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}} made of Aso welded tuff (known as Aso Pink Stone) was discovered. This coffin, with its four protrusions for rope attachments, represents the easternmost example of such coffins{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Artifacts recorded during the Genroku excavation included a crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, and mirrors. However, these were also reburied alongside the coffin{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The tomb is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, during the mid-Kofun period. It follows the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun chronologically and predates the Kakigome Kofun{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. While it has been designated as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, this attribution is considered unlikely due to discrepancies in the timeline{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. == Excavation History == * Genroku 9 (1696): Discovery of a stone coffin and associated artifacts during temple renovations. Relics reburied at the Hori Residence{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1872 (Meiji 5): Inspection by the Ministry of Education, with sketches made of stone coffin fragments{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1874 (Meiji 7): Official designation as Okinaga Tomb by the Ministry of Education{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1875 (Meiji 8): Mound construction at reburial site, officially recognized as a tomb{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1883 (Meiji 16): Former burial site brought under Imperial Household Agency jurisdiction{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. While it has been designated as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, this attribution is considered unlikely due to discrepancies in the timeline{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. == Excavation History == * Genroku 9 (1696): Discovery of a stone coffin and associated artifacts during temple renovations. Relics reburied at the Hori Residence{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1872 (Meiji 5): Inspection by the Ministry of Education, with sketches made of stone coffin fragments{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1874 (Meiji 7): Official designation as Okinaga Tomb by the Ministry of Education{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1875 (Meiji 8): Mound construction at reburial site, officially recognized as a tomb{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1883 (Meiji 16): Former burial site brought under Imperial Household Agency jurisdiction{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Remains of the mound (Auxiliary area of Okinaga Tomb) |alternate_name = Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Kofun{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}/Kōgōzuka{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}} |part_of = Nagahama Kofun Cluster |location = {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, Shiga Prefecture, Muraida Aza Kitayashiki |coordinates = 35.4014667 |coordinates = 136.3363611 |omitted = JP-25 |type = (Hypothetical) {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tomb|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} |map_size = Unknown |Construction = Mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |burial = Unknown (contains a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}}) |Buried Individual = (Designated by {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}} |burial goods = Crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, mirror, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |Mausoleum = Designated as "Okinaga Tomb" by the Imperial Household Agency |Notes = |map = Japan Shiga |map_dot_label = Muraida Kofun |type = |map_dot_label = top }} [[File:Okinaga-no-Misasagi, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Shrine}}{{small|Relics reburied in Genroku era; mound constructed and designation1 as a tomb in Meiji era.}}]] '''Muraida Kofun''' (村居田古墳) is an ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, Shiga Prefecture. Its type is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, and it is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster. The actual individual buried in the tomb remains unclear. However, part of the site has been designation1 by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, empress of Emperor Bidatsu, the 30th {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. == Overview == [[File:Kouunji (Maibara), hondou.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple}}{{small|Mound remains are adjacent to the temple's main hall.}}]] Muraida Kofun is situated in the northeastern part of Shiga Prefecture, near the northern end of the Yokoyama Hills in the Hokoku region{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. It is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster (Yokoyama Northern Kofun Cluster), which includes tombs such as the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun and the Kakigome Kofun in Nagahama City{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. According to the *Sakata District Gazetteer* (*Sakata-gunshi*), the mound was discovered in Genroku 9 (1696) during renovations to the main hall of Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple. A stone coffin was unearthed from the mound but later reburied at the current tomb site (designation1 as Okinaga Tomb). During this discovery, much of the original mound was destroyed{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}, and the remaining parts of the mound are now managed by the Imperial Household Agency. No full-map_size investigations have been conducted due to this designation{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The mound's exact type is unclear due to erosion, but it is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. Surrounding the site, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} (cylindrical haniwa) have been found{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Inside the mound, a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}} made of Aso welded tuff (known as Aso Pink Stone) was discovered. This coffin, with its four protrusions for rope attachments, represents the easternmost example of such coffins{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. burial goods recorded during the Genroku excavation included a crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, and mirrors. However, these were also reburied alongside the coffin{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The tomb is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, during the mid-Kofun period. It follows the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun chronologically and predates the Kakigome Kofun{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. While it has been designation1 as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, this attribution is considered unlikely due to discrepancies in the timeline{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. == Excavation History == * Genroku 9 (1696): Discovery of a stone coffin and associated burial goods during temple renovations. Relics reburied at the Hori Residence{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1872 (Meiji 5): Inspection by the Ministry of Education, with sketches made of stone coffin fragments{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1874 (Meiji 7): Official designation as Okinaga Tomb by the Ministry of Education{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1875 (Meiji 8): Mound construction at reburial site, officially recognized as a tomb{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1883 (Meiji 16): Former burial site brought under Imperial Household Agency jurisdiction{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=trimming these off. the parameter works as "burial" so no need to use "Burial Facility"[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:35, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=added parameters Interred_individual and Mausoleum is planned to be added but I messed up in template editing[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:40, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=now we have a 'burial' and 'burial goods' parameter. So a lot of these pages might not be marked on our wiki as having unknown parameters. But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:02, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones[[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Ancient Japanese Tomb |name = Muraida Kofun |image = [[File:Okinaga-no-Misasagi, zankyu.jpg|280px]]
Remains of the mound (Auxiliary area of Okinaga Tomb) |alternate_name = Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Kofun{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}/Kōgōzuka{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}} |part_of = Nagahama Kofun Cluster |location = {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, Shiga Prefecture, Muraida Aza Kitayashiki |coordinates = 35.4014667 |coordinates = 136.3363611 |omitted = JP-25 |type = (Hypothetical) {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tomb|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} |map_size = Unknown |Construction = Mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |burial = Unknown (contains a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}}) |Buried Individual = (Designated by {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}} |burial goods = Crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, mirror, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |Mausoleum = Designated as "Okinaga Tomb" by the Imperial Household Agency |notes = |map = Japan Shiga |map_dot_label = Muraida Kofun |type = |map_dot_label = top }} [[File:Okinaga-no-Misasagi, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Shrine}}{{small|Relics reburied in Genroku era; mound constructed and designation1 as a tomb in Meiji era.}}]] '''Muraida Kofun''' (村居田古墳) is an ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, Shiga Prefecture. Its type is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, and it is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster. The actual individual buried in the tomb remains unclear. However, part of the site has been designation1 by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, empress of Emperor Bidatsu, the 30th {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. == Overview == [[File:Kouunji (Maibara), hondou.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple}}{{small|Mound remains are adjacent to the temple's main hall.}}]] Muraida Kofun is situated in the northeastern part of Shiga Prefecture, near the northern end of the Yokoyama Hills in the Hokoku region{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. It is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster (Yokoyama Northern Kofun Cluster), which includes tombs such as the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun and the Kakigome Kofun in Nagahama City{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. According to the *Sakata District Gazetteer* (*Sakata-gunshi*), the mound was discovered in Genroku 9 (1696) during renovations to the main hall of Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple. A stone coffin was unearthed from the mound but later reburied at the current tomb site (designation1 as Okinaga Tomb). During this discovery, much of the original mound was destroyed{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}, and the remaining parts of the mound are now managed by the Imperial Household Agency. No full-map_size investigations have been conducted due to this designation{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The mound's exact type is unclear due to erosion, but it is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. Surrounding the site, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} (cylindrical haniwa) have been found{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Inside the mound, a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}} made of Aso welded tuff (known as Aso Pink Stone) was discovered. This coffin, with its four protrusions for rope attachments, represents the easternmost example of such coffins{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. burial goods recorded during the Genroku excavation included a crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, and mirrors. However, these were also reburied alongside the coffin{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The tomb is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, during the mid-Kofun period. It follows the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun chronologically and predates the Kakigome Kofun{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. While it has been designation1 as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, this attribution is considered unlikely due to discrepancies in the timeline{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. == Excavation History == * Genroku 9 (1696): Discovery of a stone coffin and associated burial goods during temple renovations. Relics reburied at the Hori Residence{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1872 (Meiji 5): Inspection by the Ministry of Education, with sketches made of stone coffin fragments{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1874 (Meiji 7): Official designation as Okinaga Tomb by the Ministry of Education{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1875 (Meiji 8): Mound construction at reburial site, officially recognized as a tomb{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1883 (Meiji 16): Former burial site brought under Imperial Household Agency jurisdiction{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Shosuke Ishida|year=|chapter=Okinaga Tomb|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Era)|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|ref={{Harvid|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Fuminori Sugaya|year=2002|chapter=Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group|title={{ill|Zoku Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|続 日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|isbn=4490105991|ref={{Harvid|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Tetsuro Tsujikawa|year=2006|chapter=Reevaluation of Muraida Kofun in Maibara City|title=Shiga other_designation Protection Association Annual Report Vol. 19|publisher=Shiga other_designation Protection Association|ref={{Harvid|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Chiaki Mizutani|year=2013|chapter=|title=The Mystery of Emperor Keitai and the Korean Peninsula|publisher=Bunshun Shinsho|isbn=978-4166609253|ref={{Harvid|Chiaki Mizutani|2013}}}} == See Also == {{Commonscat|Muraida Kofun}} * {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}} [[Category:Kofun in Shiga Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Maibara]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Imperial mausolea of Japan]] [[Category:completion tier 4]][[Category: tier 6]][[Category: tier 4]][[sn:Muraita Kofun]][[Category:completion tier Kofun]][[sn:Muraita Kofun]][[sn:Muraita Kofun]][[sn:Muraita Kofun]][[Category:11]][[sn:Muraita Kofun]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Muraita Kofun]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Muraita Kofun]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Muraita Kofun]][[Category:11]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Muraita Kofun}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Muraita Kofun]][[Category:qq]] It is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster (Yokoyama Northern Kofun Cluster), which includes tombs such as the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun and the Kakigome Kofun in Nagahama City{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. According to the *Sakata District Gazetteer* (*Sakata-gunshi*), the mound was discovered in Genroku 9 (1696) during renovations to the main hall of Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple. A stone coffin was unearthed from the mound but later reburied at the current tomb site (other_designation1 as Okinaga Tomb). During this discovery, much of the original mound was destroyed{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}, and the remaining parts of the mound are now managed by the Imperial Household Agency. No full-map_size investigations have been conducted due to this other_designation{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The mound's exact type is unclear due to erosion, but it is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. Surrounding the site, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} (cylindrical haniwa) have been found{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Inside the mound, a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}} made of Aso welded tuff (known as Aso Pink Stone) was discovered. This coffin, with its four protrusions for rope attachments, represents the easternmost example of such coffins{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. burial goods recorded during the Genroku excavation included a crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, and mirrors. However, these were also reburied alongside the coffin{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The tomb is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, during the mid-Kofun period. It follows the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun chronologically and predates the Kakigome Kofun{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. While it has been other_designation1 as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, this attribution is considered unlikely due to discrepancies in the timeline{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. == Excavation History == * Genroku 9 (1696): Discovery of a stone coffin and associated burial goods during temple renovations. Relics reburied at the Hori Residence{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1872 (Meiji 5): Inspection by the Ministry of Education, with sketches made of stone coffin fragments{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1874 (Meiji 7): Official other_designation as Okinaga Tomb by the Ministry of Education{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1875 (Meiji 8): Mound construction at reburial site, officially recognized as a tomb{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1883 (Meiji 16): Former burial site brought under Imperial Household Agency jurisdiction{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Shosuke Ishida|year=|chapter=Okinaga Tomb|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa {{afc comment|1=trimming these off. the parameter works as "burial" so no need to use "Burial Facility"[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:35, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=added parameters Interred_individual and Mausoleum is planned to be added but I messed up in template editing[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:40, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=now we have a 'burial' and 'burial goods' parameter. So a lot of these pages might not be marked on our wiki as having unknown parameters. But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:02, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones[[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Ancient Japanese Tomb |name = Muraida Kofun |image = [[File:Okinaga-no-Misasagi, zankyu.jpg|280px]]
Remains of the mound (Auxiliary area of Okinaga Tomb) |alternate_name = Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Kofun{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}/Kōgōzuka{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}} |part_of = Nagahama Kofun Cluster |location = {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, Shiga Prefecture, Muraida Aza Kitayashiki |coordinates = 35.4014667 |coordinates = 136.3363611 |omitted = JP-25 |type = (Hypothetical) {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tomb|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} |map_size = Unknown |Construction = Mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |burial = Unknown (contains a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}}) |Buried Individual = (Designated by {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}} |burial goods = Crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, mirror, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |Mausoleum = Designated as "Okinaga Tomb" by the Imperial Household Agency |notes = |map = Japan Shiga |map_dot_label = Muraida Kofun |type = |map_dot_label = top }} [[File:Okinaga-no-Misasagi, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Shrine}}{{small|Relics reburied in Genroku era; mound constructed and other_designation1 as a tomb in Meiji era.}}]] '''Muraida Kofun''' (村居田古墳) is an ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, Shiga Prefecture. Its type is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, and it is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster. The actual individual buried in the tomb remains unclear. However, part of the site has been other_designation1 by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, empress of Emperor Bidatsu, the 30th {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. == Overview == [[File:Kouunji (Maibara), hondou.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple}}{{small|Mound remains are adjacent to the temple's main hall.}}]] Muraida Kofun is situated in the northeastern part of Shiga Prefecture, near the northern end of the Yokoyama Hills in the Hokoku region{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. It is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster (Yokoyama Northern Kofun Cluster), which includes tombs such as the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun and the Kakigome Kofun in Nagahama City{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. According to the *Sakata District Gazetteer* (*Sakata-gunshi*), the mound was discovered in Genroku 9 (1696) during renovations to the main hall of Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple. A stone coffin was unearthed from the mound but later reburied at the current tomb site (other_designation1 as Okinaga Tomb). During this discovery, much of the original mound was destroyed{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}, and the remaining parts of the mound are now managed by the Imperial Household Agency. No full-map_size investigations have been conducted due to this other_designation{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The mound's exact type is unclear due to erosion, but it is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. Surrounding the site, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} (cylindrical haniwa) have been found{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Inside the mound, a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}} made of Aso welded tuff (known as Aso Pink Stone) was discovered. This coffin, with its four protrusions for rope attachments, represents the easternmost example of such coffins{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. burial goods recorded during the Genroku excavation included a crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, and mirrors. However, these were also reburied alongside the coffin{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The tomb is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, during the mid-Kofun period. It follows the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun chronologically and predates the Kakigome Kofun{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. While it has been other_designation1 as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, this attribution is considered unlikely due to discrepancies in the timeline{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. == Excavation History == * Genroku 9 (1696): Discovery of a stone coffin and associated burial goods during temple renovations. Relics reburied at the Hori Residence{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1872 (Meiji 5): Inspection by the Ministry of Education, with sketches made of stone coffin fragments{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1874 (Meiji 7): Official other_designation as Okinaga Tomb by the Ministry of Education{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1875 (Meiji 8): Mound construction at reburial site, officially recognized as a tomb{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1883 (Meiji 16): Former burial site brought under Imperial Household Agency jurisdiction{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Shosuke Ishida|year=|chapter=Okinaga Tomb|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Era)|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|ref={{Harvid|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Fuminori Sugaya|year=2002|chapter=Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group|title={{ill|Zoku Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|続 日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|isbn=4490105991|ref={{Harvid|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Tetsuro Tsujikawa|year=2006|chapter=Reevaluation of Muraida Kofun in Maibara City|title=Shiga other_designation Protection Association Annual Report Vol. 19|publisher=Shiga other_designation Protection Association|ref={{Harvid|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Chiaki Mizutani|year=2013|chapter=|title=The Mystery of Emperor Keitai and the Korean Peninsula|publisher=Bunshun Shinsho|isbn=978-4166609253|ref={{Harvid|Chiaki Mizutani|2013}}}} == See Also == {{Commonscat|Muraida Kofun}} * {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}} [[Category:Kofun in Shiga {{afc comment|1=trimming these off. the parameter works as "burial" so no need to use "Burial Facility"[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:35, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=added parameters Interred_individual and Mausoleum is planned to be added but I messed up in template editing[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:40, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=now we have a 'burial' and 'burial goods' parameter. So a lot of these pages might not be marked on our wiki as having unknown parameters. But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:02, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones[[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Ancient Japanese Tomb |name = Muraida Kofun |image = [[File:Okinaga-no-Misasagi, zankyu.jpg|280px]]
Remains of the mound (Auxiliary area of Okinaga Tomb) |alternate_name = Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Kofun{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}/Kōgōzuka{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}} |part_of = Nagahama Kofun Cluster |location = {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, Shiga Prefecture, Muraida Aza Kitayashiki |coordinates = 35.4014667 |coordinates = 136.3363611 |omitted = JP-25 |type = (Hypothetical) {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tomb|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} |map_size = Unknown |Construction = Mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |burial = Unknown (contains a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}}) |Buried Individual = (Designated by {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}} |burial goods = Crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, mirror, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |Mausoleum = Designated as "Okinaga Tomb" by the Imperial Household Agency |notes = |map = Japan Shiga |map_dot_label = Muraida Kofun |type = |map_dot_label = top }} [[File:Okinaga-no-Misasagi, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Shrine}}{{small|Relics reburied in Genroku era; mound constructed and other_designation1 as a tomb in Meiji era.}}]] '''Muraida Kofun''' (村居田古墳) is an ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in {{ill|Maibara|ja|米原市}}, Shiga Prefecture. Its type is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, and it is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster. The actual individual buried in the tomb remains unclear. However, part of the site has been other_designation1 by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, empress of Emperor Bidatsu, the 30th {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}. == Overview == [[File:Kouunji (Maibara), hondou.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple}}{{small|Mound remains are adjacent to the temple's main hall.}}]] Muraida Kofun is situated in the northeastern part of Shiga Prefecture, near the northern end of the Yokoyama Hills in the Hokoku region{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. It is part of the Nagahama Kofun Cluster (Yokoyama Northern Kofun Cluster), which includes tombs such as the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun and the Kakigome Kofun in Nagahama City{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. According to the *Sakata District Gazetteer* (*Sakata-gunshi*), the mound was discovered in Genroku 9 (1696) during renovations to the main hall of Araryōzan Kōun-ji Temple. A stone coffin was unearthed from the mound but later reburied at the current tomb site (other_designation1 as Okinaga Tomb). During this discovery, much of the original mound was destroyed{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}, and the remaining parts of the mound are now managed by the Imperial Household Agency. No full-map_size investigations have been conducted due to this other_designation{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The mound's exact type is unclear due to erosion, but it is hypothesized to be {{ill|keyhole-shaped|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. Surrounding the site, fragments of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} (cylindrical haniwa) have been found{{Sfn|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. Inside the mound, a {{ill|house-shaped stone coffin|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}} made of Aso welded tuff (known as Aso Pink Stone) was discovered. This coffin, with its four protrusions for rope attachments, represents the easternmost example of such coffins{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. burial goods recorded during the Genroku excavation included a crown, {{ill|sword|ja|大刀}}, and mirrors. However, these were also reburied alongside the coffin{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. The tomb is estimated to have been constructed in the mid-to-late {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, during the mid-Kofun period. It follows the Nagahama Chausuyama Kofun chronologically and predates the Kakigome Kofun{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}{{Sfn|Chiaki Mizutani|2013|pp=76–97}}. While it has been other_designation1 as the tomb of {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}}, this attribution is considered unlikely due to discrepancies in the timeline{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Heibonsha)|1991}}. == Excavation History == * Genroku 9 (1696): Discovery of a stone coffin and associated burial goods during temple renovations. Relics reburied at the Hori Residence{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1872 (Meiji 5): Inspection by the Ministry of Education, with sketches made of stone coffin fragments{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. * 1874 (Meiji 7): Official other_designation as Okinaga Tomb by the Ministry of Education{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1875 (Meiji 8): Mound construction at reburial site, officially recognized as a tomb{{Sfn|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}. * 1883 (Meiji 16): Former burial site brought under Imperial Household Agency jurisdiction{{Sfn|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Shosuke Ishida|year=|chapter=Okinaga Tomb|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Era)|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|ref={{Harvid|Okinaga Tomb (Kokushi)}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Fuminori Sugaya|year=2002|chapter=Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group|title={{ill|Zoku Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|続 日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|isbn=4490105991|ref={{Harvid|Hirohime Okinaga Tomb Group (Zoku Kofun)|2002}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Tetsuro Tsujikawa|year=2006|chapter=Reevaluation of Muraida Kofun in Maibara City|title=Shiga other_designation Protection Association Annual Report Vol. 19|publisher=Shiga other_designation Protection Association|ref={{Harvid|Tetsuro Tsujikawa|2006}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Chiaki Mizutani|year=2013|chapter=|title=The Mystery of Emperor Keitai and the Korean Peninsula|publisher=Bunshun Shinsho|isbn=978-4166609253|ref={{Harvid|Chiaki Mizutani|2013}}}} == See Also == {{Commonscat|Muraida Kofun}} * {{ill|Hirohime|ja|広姫}} [[Category:Kofun in Shiga Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Maibara]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Imperial mausolea of Japan]] [[Category:completion tier 4]][[Category: tier 6]][[Category: tier 4]][[sn:Muraita
